config_files.notes: Rust uses a custom cfg format (key value, no equals sign). This file is the primary graphics config surface and is NOT overwritten by the in-game menu — it is the authoritative source. Values set here override in-game defaults on startup.

keys.effect: Overall graphics quality preset: 0=lowest, 1=low, 2=medium, 3=high, 4=ultra. Sets a baseline for most sub-settings.

keys.notes: Disabling shadows (0) is the single highest FPS gain in Rust and a common competitive choice. Also affects gameplay visibility — players in shadows are harder to see with shadows disabled.

keys.effect: Shadow render distance in metres. Default 100.
Terrain and object draw distance multiplier.
Field of view in degrees. Default 90.
keys.notes: Rust allows FOV up to 90°. Increasing FOV reduces per-frame rendering cost slightly at the edges but provides better situational awareness. Adjust for user preference within the 70-90° range.
Anisotropic filtering has minimal performance cost on modern GPUs and significantly improves terrain texture clarity at oblique angles. Recommend minimum 4 at all tiers.

manual_only_settings.recommendation: 0 on performance hardware. Gibs (debris/ragdoll fragments from deaths and explosions) accumulate in scenes and cause progressive frame time increases during active raids.

notes: Rust's client.cfg is the authoritative config file — values here persist and override in-game settings on startup. This is the opposite of most other games where the in-game menu overwrites the config.
Rust is heavily CPU-bound during base raids and large-server events. Shadow distance and draw distance have greater FPS impact than most GPU settings in these scenarios.
Max Gibs accumulation is a well-known progressive performance issue in Rust — it should be one of the first recommendations for players experiencing decreasing FPS over a play session.
Rust's anti-cheat (EAC — EasyAntiCheat) does not scan client.cfg. All keys listed here are EAC-safe.
